channel.title: Silver Score
channel.description: Cinema magazine and watchlist — curated lists, essays, and honest scores from ~8,500+ hours of film and TV.

item.title: Essay: Why We Rewatch Michael Mann
item.description: Professionalism as drama, cities as pressure systems, and why Michael Mann's shelf keeps rewarding the rewatch.

item.title: Essay: How We Find Under-the-Radar Gems
item.description: The method behind the shelf — the 50k-vote line, three recurring patterns, and what we actually look for.

item.title: Essay: Subtitles, Dubbing, and What Gets Lost
item.description: Dubbing swaps an actor's instrument for a session performer. Subtitles ask a little work and give you back the performance the director actually shot.

item.title: Essay: The Limited Series Is the New Prestige Film
item.description: Six to eight episodes have absorbed the craft energy that used to define mid-budget prestige film.

item.title: Essay: The French Polar — Noir That Never Quit
item.description: France kept its parallel noir tradition running without interruption, from Melville through Spiral and Le Bureau des Légendes.

item.title: New list: International Thrillers — Beyond English
item.description: The best tension doesn't need a shared language. Thrillers from France, Germany, Israel, India, and beyond.

item.title: New list: Slow Burns Worth the Patience
item.description: Films and series that trust the audience enough to take their time — tension built from craft, not jump cuts.

item.title: New list: Korean Cinema — Sharp Edges
item.description: Revenge, class warfare, and genre-bending that Hollywood still studies.

item.title: New list: War Films That Earned It
item.description: No glory montages. Films and series where war costs something — told with craft, restraint, and respect.

item.title: New list: Italian Crime — No Heroes Allowed
item.description: Naples, Rome, Calabria — Italian crime TV and film where the system is the villain and nobody gets a redemption arc.

item.title: Essay: In Defence of Slow Burns
item.description: Why the best thrillers make you wait. A case for patience, quiet tension, and stories that respect the audience.

item.title: Essay: The Craft of Spy TV
item.description: From The Bureau to Slow Horses: what separates great espionage television from action shows in trench coats.

item.title: Essay: Why Nordic Noir Works
item.description: Landscape as character, silence as dialogue, and detectives who carry the weight of the case home.

item.title: Curated list: Prestige TV That Earned the Label
item.description: Series that justified the late nights — high craft, sharp writing, and finales we still argue about.

item.title: Curated list: Spy & Thriller Escape Room
item.description: Agencies, aliases, and anxiety — the shows we return to when we want people who are scary-good at hard jobs.

item.title: Curated list: Nordic Noir & Neighbours
item.description: Slow-burn crime from the north — moral grey zones, landscapes as character, and detectives who look tired in the right way.
